sections.heading: Market sizing and segmentation
sections.body_html:
The Vietnamese fintech market reached an estimated USD 52 bn in digital payment transaction volume in 2025, representing a 27% year-on-year increase from 2024. Five segments make up the addressable opportunity: payments (the dominant segment at ~68% of revenue), lending, wealth management, insurance distribution, and infrastructure / B2B fintech.

Forecasts for 2026 vary across operators we interviewed, but a consensus band of USD 60-65 bn transaction volume is reasonable given current run-rates, with lending the fastest-growing segment in revenue terms.

sections.heading: Competitive landscape
sections.body_html:
Three operators currently dominate the consumer-facing payments layer: MoMo, ZaloPay, and VNPay. MoMo holds the largest active user base (~31M monthly transacting users as of Q1 2026), while ZaloPay's strength lies in its Zalo integration moat and VNPay leads in merchant acceptance breadth.

Beyond the consumer trio, we tracked notable activity from Cake by VPBank (digital-first banking), Timo (acquired by Phenikaa), and three emerging neobank entrants with regulatory progress in 2025-26. Cross-border players (Wise, Remitly) remain marginal in the consumer flow but meaningful in SME corridors.

sections.heading: Channel and distribution dynamics
sections.body_html:
The channel story has bifurcated. QR-based payments now account for ~58% of consumer transactions (up from 41% in 2023), while account-to-account (A2A) via NAPAS rails has stabilised around 28%. Card-present transactions continue their secular decline.

For merchants, the unit economics of acquiring shifted meaningfully in 2025: blended merchant discount rates compressed by ~14 bps on average as competitive pressure from QR providers forced traditional acquirers to match. We expect further compression in 2026 as super-app operators bundle payments with credit and loyalty.

sections.heading: AI impact on the sector
sections.body_html:
The most significant 2026 development is not a new product, it's the cost-structure shift among operators who have fully integrated AI into operations. Leading operators report:

  • 22-38% cost-per-transaction declines driven by automated customer service, smart routing, and reduced manual review on lending decisions
  • ~18% expansion in addressable lending market from AI-augmented underwriting that incorporates non-traditional cash-flow signals
  • Measurable chargeback reduction across the top three payment rails from improved fraud detection

Banks pursuing the same playbook face a structural disadvantage: they lack the in-product cash-flow data the super-apps capture natively. The competitive moat isn't the model — it's the data the model is fed.

sections.heading: Strategic implications
sections.body_html:
For incumbents — both Vietnamese commercial banks and regional fintech challengers — three strategic responses are credible in 2026:

  1. Acquire the data layer: partner with payment platforms, negotiate data-sharing rights, or move into payment acceptance to capture the same signals
  2. Compete on segments super-apps don't cover: cross-border flows, project finance, secured lending, and the larger ticket sizes (above VND 500M) remain available
  3. Verticalise into specific industries: F&B, logistics, and SME services each present opportunities where dedicated fintech wrapping a vertical software stack can outcompete the horizontal super-apps

The strategy that doesn't work is pretending the gap is technological. Several incumbent retail banks made that mistake in 2024-25 and shed measurable market share through it.
